tasks.title: SQL Generation
tasks.summary: Generate SQL for a natural-language question against a fixed schema. Graded by executing the SQL and comparing result rows to a reference query.
tasks.tierDetail: Deterministic: the generated SQL is run against an in-memory SQLite database seeded from a known schema, and its result set is compared (row-for-row) to the reference query's result. No LLM judges the answer.

tasks.title: Code Explanation
tasks.summary: Explain what a short function does. Graded by keyword coverage against a reference set of concepts the explanation must mention.
tasks.tierDetail: Reference-based: the explanation is scored on the fraction of required concept keywords it mentions (case-insensitive). It passes at ≥70% coverage. Weaker than execution, but objective and judge-free.

tasks.title: Document Extraction
tasks.summary: Extract named fields from a short document as JSON. Graded by field-level F1 against an exact expected object.
tasks.tierDetail: Reference-based: the returned JSON is compared field-by-field to an expected object (case-insensitive, trimmed). Score is the F1 of correctly extracted fields; it passes only at a perfect F1 of 1.0.

cases.prompt:
Explain what this function does:

function f(s) { return s.split('').reverse().join(''); }
Extract invoice_number, total, and due_date as JSON.
Extract name, company, and phone as JSON.
Extract event, date, and location as JSON.
Extract product, price, and weight as JSON.

gpt-frontier.prompt: Return the top 2 product categories by total line-item revenue (quantity times product price), highest revenue first. Return the category and its revenue.
gpt-frontier.expected: SELECT p.category, SUM(oi.qty * p.price) AS revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.category ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 2;
gpt-frontier.got: SELECT p.category, SUM(oi.qty * p.price) AS revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.category ORDER BY revenue ASC LIMIT 2;
gpt-frontier.analysis: Sorted ascending instead of descending, so it returned the two *lowest*-revenue categories (Stationery, Furniture) rather than the top two. The join and aggregation were correct — only the ORDER BY direction was wrong, a subtle slip that deterministic execution catches immediately.

gpt-frontier.prompt: Extract event, date, and location as JSON.
gpt-frontier.analysis: Extracted only the city ('Seattle') for location, dropping the venue ('Harbor Center'). The expected value is the full 'Harbor Center, Seattle', so the field is counted wrong — a partial extraction that an exact-match grader treats as a miss.

gemini-ultra.prompt: Return the top 2 product categories by total line-item revenue (quantity times product price), highest revenue first. Return the category and its revenue.
gemini-ultra.expected: SELECT p.category, SUM(oi.qty * p.price) AS revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.category ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 2;
gemini-ultra.got: SELECT p.name, SUM(oi.qty * p.price) AS revenue FROM order_items oi JOIN products p ON p.id = oi.product_id GROUP BY p.name ORDER BY revenue DESC LIMIT 2;
gemini-ultra.analysis: Grouped by individual product (p.name) instead of product category, so it ranked products (Monitor, Desk) rather than categories. It misread the grain of the question — the answer looks plausible but aggregates at the wrong level.

gemini-ultra.prompt: List the names of customers who have never placed an order.
gemini-ultra.expected: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders);
gemini-ultra.got: SELECT name FROM customers WHERE id NOT IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders WHERE status = 'completed');
gemini-ultra.analysis: Added a status='completed' filter to the subquery, answering 'customers with no completed order' rather than 'no order at all'. This wrongly includes Dmitri, whose only order is pending. A meaning-changing over-specification.

gemini-ultra.got: A memoization helper. It stores computed results in a cache (a Map) and returns the cached value whenever the same input is requested again.
gemini-ultra.analysis: Correctly identified memoization and the cache, but never mentioned that the cache lives in a closure — the very mechanism that lets it persist across calls. Keyword coverage fell to 2/3, below the 70% bar. The explanation is right but omits the 'why it works' detail.

gemini-ultra.prompt: Extract invoice_number, total, and due_date as JSON.
gemini-ultra.analysis: Normalized the total to '1250.00', stripping the currency symbol and thousands separator the source showed ('$1,250.00'). Helpful in some pipelines, but it no longer matches the expected verbatim value, so exact-match scoring marks it wrong.

gemini-ultra.prompt: Extract event, date, and location as JSON.
gemini-ultra.analysis: Same partial-location error: returned 'Seattle' without the 'Harbor Center' venue. Two of three fields were correct, giving an F1 of 0.67 — below the perfect-match threshold.
